quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Separable DE"?

options.text: Motion along a straight line (one dimension); described completely by position as a function of time s(t)
A DE of the form dy/dx = g(x)·h(y); the x and y terms can be separated to opposite sides
Use similar triangles to relate the heights and distances of an object and its shadow; differentiate to find shadow length rate
Local min: concave up valley (f'' > 0); local max: concave down hill (f'' < 0)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Separable DE" is defined as: A DE of the form dy/dx = g(x)·h(y); the x and y terms can be separated to opposite sides. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Separation process"?

options.text: Left-hand limit: lim(x→c⁻) f(x); right-hand limit: lim(x→c⁺) f(x); the two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal
If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0: local minimum (concave up); f''(c) < 0: local maximum (concave down)
Rewrite as dy/h(y) = g(x)dx, then integrate both sides independently
Given f(a) and f(b) with continuity/differentiability: MVT guarantees f'(c) = [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) for some c between a and b

quiz_questions.explanation: "Separation process" is defined as: Rewrite as dy/h(y) = g(x)dx, then integrate both sides independently. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Implicit vs. explicit solution"?

options.text: Uses the midpoint of each subinterval: M_n = Σf((xᵢ + xᵢ₊₁)/2)Δx; generally more accurate than left/right
∫f'(x)/f(x)dx = ln|f(x)| + C (u = f(x)); ∫f'(x)·[f(x)]ⁿdx = [f(x)]ⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C
A point where the concavity of f changes; requires f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND a sign change in f''
Sometimes the result ∫dy/h(y) = ∫g(x)dx remains implicit (y not isolated); both forms are valid solutions

quiz_questions.explanation: "Implicit vs. explicit solution" is defined as: Sometimes the result ∫dy/h(y) = ∫g(x)dx remains implicit (y not isolated); both forms are valid solutions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Domain restrictions"?

options.text: Division by h(y) requires h(y) ≠ 0; check if constant solutions y = c where h(c) = 0 are also solutions
0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞-∞, 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰; L'Hôpital's directly handles the first two
Motion along a straight line (one dimension); described completely by position as a function of time s(t)
Given f(a) and f(b) with continuity/differentiability: MVT guarantees f'(c) = [f(b)-f(a)]/(b-a) for some c between a and b

quiz_questions.explanation: "Domain restrictions" is defined as: Division by h(y) requires h(y) ≠ 0; check if constant solutions y = c where h(c) = 0 are also solutions. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Initial condition application"?

options.text: Derived from the limit definition using sin(x+h) = sin x cos h + cos x sin h and special limits
d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [f'(x)g(x) - f(x)g'(x)] / [g(x)]²; 'low d-high minus high d-low over low squared'
After integrating both sides (with one constant C), substitute (x₀, y₀) to find C
For definite integrals: convert limits from x to u (u_lower = g(a), u_upper = g(b)); no need to substitute back

quiz_questions.explanation: "Initial condition application" is defined as: After integrating both sides (with one constant C), substitute (x₀, y₀) to find C. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A DE of the form dy/dx = g(x)·h(y); the x and y terms can be separated to opposite sides"?

options.text: Comparison with first derivative test
Equilibrium solutions
Separable DE
Extreme Value Theorem

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Separable DE".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Rewrite as dy/h(y) = g(x)dx, then integrate both sides independently"?

options.text: Variable exponent functions
Separation process
Multiple chain rule
Conservative forces

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Separation process".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Sometimes the result ∫dy/h(y) = ∫g(x)dx remains implicit (y not isolated); both forms are valid solutions"?

options.text: Integration by parts indicators
Implicit vs. explicit solution
Displacement vs. distance
Logistic DE

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Implicit vs. explicit solution".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Division by h(y) requires h(y) ≠ 0; check if constant solutions y = c where h(c) = 0 are also solutions"?

options.text: Common quotient rule errors
Domain restrictions
Second derivative test
Angular rate problems

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Domain restrictions".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "After integrating both sides (with one constant C), substitute (x₀, y₀) to find C"?

options.text: Acceleration
Epsilon (ε)
Angular rate problems
Initial condition application

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Initial condition application".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Separation of Variables is accurate?

options.text: An infinite limit lim(x→c) f(x) = ±∞ means f(x) increases or decreases without bound as x approaches c.
Formal Definition of Limits (Epsilon-Delta) provides the rigorous mathematical foundation for limits.
Separation of Variables is the primary method for solving first-order DEs of the form dy/dx = g(x)h(y).
The horizontal line test determines if a function is one-to-one.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Separation of Variables as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Separation of Variables?

options.text: Solutions are functions y(x) that satisfy the equation.
The technique: (1) separate variables: dy/h(y) = g(x)dx, (2) integrate both sides: ∫dy/h(y) = ∫g(x)dx, (3) solve for y if possible, (4) apply initial condition.
Mean Value Theorem: Problem Solving applies MVT to solve specific AP-style problems.
Integration by parts may need to be applied more than once.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Separation of Variables as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Separation of Variables?

options.text: Solution curves follow the direction of these segments.
Limits and Continuity are the foundation of calculus.
The sign of the derivative indicates whether the quantity is increasing or decreasing, and the second derivative reveals acceleration or deceleration of change.
This method works only when the right side factors into a product of a function of x alone and a function of y alone.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Separation of Variables as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Separation of Variables?

options.text: Many important physical models yield separable DEs.
If f'(t) is the rate of water flowing into a tank, then ∫₀ᵀ f'(t)dt is the net change in water volume.
Arc length of y = f(x) from a to b: L = ∫ₐᵇ √(1 + [f'(x)]²)dx.
The derivative of an inverse function is (f⁻¹)'(a) = 1/f'(f⁻¹(a)), derived from the chain rule applied to f(f⁻¹(x)) = x.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Separation of Variables as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Separation of Variables?

options.text: The sum, difference, product, quotient, and power laws allow limits to be computed piece by piece.
Phase line analysis reveals stability: P = 0 is unstable (arrows point away), P = K is stable (arrows point toward).
The theorem fails if the interval is open (extrema may not be attained), the function is not continuous (could have gaps), or the domain is unbounded.
Separation of Variables is the primary method for solving first-order DEs of the form dy/dx = g(x)h(y).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Separation of Variables as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Separation of Variables?

options.text: Error analysis in numerical methods: understanding limits helps predict how rounding errors accumulate as step sizes approach zero in computer calculations
AP exam problems frequently ask students to apply MVT to piecewise data: 'Given the table, must there be a value c where f'(c) = 3?'
Radioactive decay: dN/dt = -λN separates to dN/N = -λdt, giving N = N₀e^(-λt)—the exponential decay model
Surveillance camera tracking: as a car drives past a fixed camera, the rate of angle change dθ/dt determines how fast the camera must pan to keep the car in frame

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Separation of Variables.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Separation of Variables?

options.text: Battery charge level: if I(t) is the current flow, the charge Q(x) = ∫₀ˣ I(t)dt represents the accumulated charge at time x
Velocity as the derivative of position: v(t) = s'(t) tells you the instantaneous speed and direction of a moving object at any moment
Cooling/heating: Newton's law of cooling dT/dt = -k(T-Tₛ) is separable, giving T(t) = Tₛ + (T₀-Tₛ)e^(-kt)
Mountain hiking: the highest point on a trail (absolute max) and the lowest point (absolute min) are found by checking peaks, valleys, and the trail endpoints

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Separation of Variables.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Separation of Variables applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Species population modeling: deer populations in managed forests follow logistic growth, with K determined by food and habitat availability
Chemical kinetics: first-order reaction rate d[A]/dt = -k[A] separates to give [A] = [A]₀e^(-kt), modeling reactant concentration over time
Submarine hull design: calculating pressure at operating depth determines hull thickness needed to prevent collapse
Speedometer readings as instantaneous velocity: the derivative of position with respect to time at each moment, computed electronically in modern vehicles

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Separation of Variables.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Separation of Variables?

options.text: Control systems engineering: inverse tangent functions model phase shifts in frequency response, with derivatives indicating sensitivity to frequency changes
Radioactive decay: dN/dt = -λN separates to dN/N = -λdt, giving N = N₀e^(-λt)—the exponential decay model
Profit maximization: after finding the critical point of P(x), checking P''(x) < 0 confirms it's a maximum profit level, not a minimum
Computing moments of inertia in physics: ∫x²·f(x)dx often requires integration by parts, essential for structural and mechanical engineering

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Separation of Variables.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Separation of Variables in practice?

options.text: Estimating total water consumption from flow rate measurements taken at regular intervals—a practical Riemann sum application in resource management
Total rainfall accumulation: if r(t) is the rainfall rate at time t, then total accumulated rainfall by time x is R(x) = ∫₀ˣ r(t)dt
Rocket propulsion: as fuel is burned, the rocket becomes lighter, changing the force equation—integration handles the continuously varying mass
Cooling/heating: Newton's law of cooling dT/dt = -k(T-Tₛ) is separable, giving T(t) = Tₛ + (T₀-Tₛ)e^(-kt)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Separation of Variables.
